{"step_by_step_thinking": "Leuprorelin acetate is a gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist. Multiple documents confirm its use in androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) for prostate cancer. Document [4] states that in advanced prostate cancer, leuprolide (which is equivalent to leuprorelin acetate) is used as a palliative treatment to reduce testosterone levels, thereby minimizing the stimulatory effect on androgen-sensitive prostate cancer cells. Document [24] notes that hormonal therapy for prostate cancer typically begins with LHRH agonists or antagonists, and historically, surgical castration has been replaced by medications like leuprolide. Document [13] explicitly states that leuprolide is used in the treatment of prostate cancer, which is a form of androgen deprivation therapy. Additionally, Document [17] confirms that leuprolide is used in the treatment of prostate cancer and causes suppression of sex hormones. Although some documents mention the temporary testosterone surge with GnRH agonists, they also note that anti-androgen therapy is used to prevent this, but do not suggest that leuprolide is not used in ADT. Therefore, the evidence consistently supports that leuprorelin acetate is used as androgen deprivation therapy.", "answer_choice": "A"}
